coredata.dc:title: Electrostatic association complex of a polymer capped CdTe(S) quantum dot and a small molecule dye as a robust ratiometric fluorescence probe of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shenghong
Guo, Wenjing
Sun, Xiaohan
coredata.dc:description:
A ratiometric fluorescence probe for robust and rapid Cu2+ detection was established based on an electrostatic association complex of polyallylamine capped CdTe(S) quantum dots (CdTe(S)@PAI QDs) and a small molecule dye 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA). Both of the components are easy to make. Strongly fluorescent CdTe(S)@PAI QDs could be simply prepared through a one-step UV irradiation while TPCA could be obtained directly through the hydrothermal method. The ratiometric probe was built through mixing of both components, neither organic solvent nor other harsh conditions is involved. The as-prepared probe shows dual-emission at 525 nm and 420 nm under excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) remains constant with concentration changes of the complex up to ± 20% and a temperature variation from 20 to 50 °C, but shows significant change in the presence of Cu2+. The proposed probe exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cu2+. The fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) show good linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration range from 0.05 to 1.6 μM and the limit of detection (S/N = 3) can reach 24 nM . Its applicability was demonstrated by detecting Cu2+ ions in certified reference materials and real samples with recoveries in a range of 91.0–107.3%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Polyallylamine capped
CdTe(S) quantum dot
TPCA
Copper ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA
Ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the association complex of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA
Stability of the TPCA-CdTe@PAI QDs ratiometric fluorescent probe
Determination of Cu2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: scientific research platform of Lanzhou University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the scientific research platform of Lanzhou University, China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) image of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs.
The XRD (a) and FTIR (b) spertum of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs.
The UV–Vis spectrum of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs (a), the excitation and emission spectra of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs (b) and TPCA (c).
The emission spectra of TPCA, CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and the complex (a), the fluorescence spectra of TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI QDs before and after Cu2+ added.
The fluorescence stability of TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe against to light illumination (a) and ion strength (b).
The effect of concentration and temperature on the relative intensity of ratiometric fluorescence probe.
Influence of incubation time (a) and temperature (b) on the fluorescence change of TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI QDs with Cu2+ added.

Fluorescence spectra of TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI QDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ (a). Linear fitting curve of I
525nm
/I
420nm
vs Cu2+ concentrations over a range of 0.05–1.6 μmol·L−1 (b).
The selectivity of ratiometric fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection. The concentrations of metal ions and anions are all 10 μmol·L−1.
Illustration of the design of the ratiometric fluorescence probe based on CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA.
Comparison of different fluorescent probe for the sensing of Cu2+.
The analysis results for certified reference materials.

row.entry: Number of the certified reference material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of spiked Cu2+ ions in tap water and Yellow River water.

head.ce:title: Electrostatic association complex of a polymer capped CdTe(S) quantum dot and a small molecule dye as a robust ratiometric fluorescence probe of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A ratiometric fluorescence probe for robust and rapid Cu2+ detection was established based on an electrostatic association complex of polyallylamine capped CdTe(S) quantum dots (CdTe(S)@PAI QDs) and a small molecule dye 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA). Both of the components are easy to make. Strongly fluorescent CdTe(S)@PAI QDs could be simply prepared through a one-step UV irradiation while TPCA could be obtained directly through the hydrothermal method. The ratiometric probe was built through mixing of both components, neither organic solvent nor other harsh conditions is involved. The as-prepared probe shows dual-emission at 525 nm and 420 nm under excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) remains constant with concentration changes of the complex up to ± 20% and a temperature variation from 20 to 50 °C, but shows significant change in the presence of Cu2+. The proposed probe exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cu2+. The fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) show good linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration range from 0.05 to 1.6 μM and the limit of detection (S/N = 3) can reach 24 nM . Its applicability was demonstrated by detecting Cu2+ ions in certified reference materials and real samples with recoveries in a range of 91.0–107.3%.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an important element and plays a vital role in lots of biological processes, including antioxidant effects, energy generation, bone formation and cell respiration [1–3]. Nevertheless, copper at abnormal levels is also hazardous to plants and animals. The balance of cellular processes can be broken by excessive copper ions (Cu2+), leading to series of diseases such as nephrosis, eczema and nervous system damage [4,5]. However, elementary copper and copper compounds have been widely applied in manufacturing such as electroplating, metallurgy and other chemical process. Improper wastewater treatment and discharge has already caused the serious Cu2+ ion pollution in water system [6]. Efficient approaches for accurate detection of Cu2+ are still in high demand.

Actually there are many methods available for the sensitive detection of Cu2+, including atomic emission/absorption spectroscopy [7–9], electrochemical methods [10–13], and ion chromatography-UV-vis method [14,15]. Some complexants were also used to improve the detection specificity and sensitivity in the process of Cu2+analysis [16–18]. The above mentioned techniques are all useful for the Cu2+ determination, but the large-scale equipment request, complicated pretreatment and detection procedures also against their application in on-site analysis, particularly in remote rural areas and emergencies. As an alternative, fluorescence-based detection has been proved to be a cost-effective, simple and sensitive method for the determination of pollutant, which also was benefit for field testing through incorporate it to portable device [19–21]. Many kinds of fluorescent materials have been adopted for Cu2+ detection including organic fluorophores [22–24], carbon quantum dots [25–27], polymer nanodots [28], semiconductor quantum dots [29,30] and rare-earth fluorescence materials [31,32]. However, most of these probes are based on single response, through Cu2+-induced fluorescence enhancement or quenching, which is easily affected by the variation in the probe concentration and measurement condition. Ratiometric fluorescent probes can overcome those defects perfectly through the self-calibration of multi-fluorescence signals (two or more) and have been widely employed in chemosensors and biosensors due to the excellent anti-interference capability and stability [33,34]. Unfortunately, fluorescent materials with dual-emission are still rare because of the possible quenching caused by energy transfer between two emission bands. Therefore, composite fluorescent probes based on fluorescent nanomaterials and organic dyes have received much more attention in the ratiometric detection [33–36]. However, the ratio signal of the complex probes was easy to fluctuation due to the effect of many factors, such as concentrations, temperature and ion strength. Hence, it is still necessary to develop robust ratiometric fluorescence probe for reasonable sensing application.

On the other hand, the application of complex probes was also limited from the complexity of synthesis and coupling process. Fluorescent nanomaterials have been applied quite extensively in the detection of heavy metal ions [21,25,37,38]. Among them, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have became excellent candidate for the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence probe due to their high quantum efficiency, good photostability, and most importantly, easy manipulation of optical properties and surface modification [39]. Especially, polymer functionalized QDs can improve the capture ability of the target analytes and enhance the detection sensitivity, which were more benefit for analysis application [40]. In our previous work, photochemical method has been proven to be rapid, simple and one-step synthesis method for the preparation of polymer capped QDs [41]. A simple synthesis of a stable and strongly fluorescent molecule, 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo [3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) has been established also [42]. Combination of these two types of materials provides a potential opportunity of a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe.

Herein, we design and establish a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor through integrating green-emission polyallylamine (PAI) functionalized CdTe(S) QDs (CdTe(S)@PAI QDs), which with amino groups, and blue-emission TPCA organic fluorescence molecular, which with carboxylic groups, based on their electrostatic association complex. Due to the strong chelating ability of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs toward Cu2+, the fluorescence of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs at 525 nm can be selectively quenched while the fluorescence of blue TPCA moiety at 420 nm remains constant. Compare to other ratiometric fluorescence probes, the designed probe possesses two distinct advantages. Firstly, the polymer layer PAI over CdTe(S) core not only provides a protection shield to avoid the possible fluorescence quenching between TPCA and CdTe(S), it also acts as a binding or collecting medium for both TPCA and Cu2+ to realize the sensitive detection. Then, the stable fluorescence of TPCA ensures the robustness of the probe. The fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) remains constant with concentration changes of the complex up to ± 20% and a temperature variation from 20 to 50 °C, but shows significant change in the presence of Cu2+. Furthermore, an evident fluorescence colour change from green to cyan also can be observed clearly by naked eye, which makes sensitive and robust detection of Cu2+ possible.

Cadmium chloride, thioglycolic acid (TGA, >90%) and tellurium powder were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride (>98%) was obtained from Tianjin Institute of Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Allylamine was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide was obtained by Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, cysteamine hydrochloride and benzophenone were used as received from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Two certified reference materials GB08608 (National Institute of Metrology, Beijing, China) and GBW(E) 080039 (Second Institute of Oceanography of the State Oceanic Administration, Hangzhou, China), both are simulated natural water, were used together with real samples to validate the accuracy of our method. Ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment and the resistivity is above 18.0 MΩ/cm.

The UV 2800SPC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Sunny Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and F97Pro fluorospectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used for the measurement of UV–vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra, respectively. The lattice and size of prepared QDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) measurements were carried with an X'Pert diffractometer (Philips) and a Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrometer, respectively. Induction couple plasma (ICP, IRIS ER/S, Thermo Jarrell Ash Corporation, USA) was used for the concentration calibration of spiked Cu2+ samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTe(S)@PAI QDs was obtained through photochemical method. The precursor solution of CdTe was prepared according to Gao et al. (2012) [43]. Then, 10 mL of allylamine (1.0 mol·L−1) and 60 μL of benzophenone (0.2 g·mL−1 in ethanol) were added to the mixture under continuous N2 bubbling. Then, the mixture was exposed to UV light (3 mW·cm−2) and let it react for 30 min (Scheme 1 ). After the reaction, the obtained products were filtered through 0.45 μm inorganic membrane and stored in the dark. The TPCA was synthesized according to our previous work through one-step hydrothermal method [42].

Firstly, 3.0 μL of the TPCA solution (4 mmol·L−1) and 450 μL of the CdTe@PAI QDs solution were mixed thoroughly in 2.5 mL water solution (phosphate buffer, pH 7.5). Then Cu2+ with different concentrations was added into the ratiometric probe solution. The mixture was incubated at 40 °C for 3 min and the fluorescence signal was record at 420 nm and 525 nm with excitation of 350 nm. The fluorescent titration of the probe with other metal ions and anions including Ca2+, Na+, Al3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, S2−, NO3 −, Cl− and SO4 2− were carried following the same procedure of Cu2+.

Fluorescent CdTe(S)@PAI QDs can be obtained by one-step photochemical method directly (Scheme 1). As shown in TEM images (Fig. 1 a), the prepared products were nearly spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity and uniformity. Their diameter was about 4 nm. Furthermore, from HRTEM image (Fig. 1b), we can found clear lattice fringes of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs, which suggested highly ordered structure.

XRD and FITR also was used to characterize the information of the crystal structure and surface functional groups of the as-prepared CdTe(S)@PAI QDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, we can observe the diffraction peaks of (111), (220) and (311) planes clearly, which indicate a face-centered cubic structure. The result is also consistent with the CdTe(S) QDs reported by Gao et al. (2012) [43]. This structure is also similar to polyacrylamide coated CdTe(S) prepared with the same method [41], which indicates that the amine group on the allylamine monomer causes negligible effect on the formation of CdTe(S) cores.

In FTIR spectrum of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs (Fig. 2b), stretching and bending vibration peaks of -NH2 at 3423.6 and 1571.5 cm−1 are clear. The peak at 1384.5 cm−1 can be assigned to the -CH2 of PAI moiety. Above results suggest that the CdTe(S) has been coated by PAI successfully. Polymer layer of PAI is critical for the proposed sensor because it protects CdTe(S) moiety and significantly increase its stability. NH2 groups on the outer layer provide cationic groups to form association complex with TPCA, and also facilitate the binding of Cu2+, ensuring the sensitivity of the probe. Due to the electrostatic attraction, anionic TPCA may be concentrated in the PAI layer that can guarantee the robustness of the ratiometric probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe based on the association complex of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CdTe(S)@PAI QDs show obvious absorption at about 470 nm (Fig. 3 a), which is similar to polyacrylamide coated QDs reported previously [41]. CdTe(S)@PAI QDs displayed bright green fluorescence (maximum at 525 nm) under the excitation of 360 nm (Fig. 3b) while TPCA emitted bright blue fluorescence (420 nm) under the excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 3c). Due to the amine groups on CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and carboxylic groups on TPCA, association complex can form because of the electrostatic force and a ratiometric fluorescent probe is therefore established by simple mixing of appropriate amounts of two components. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PL intensity of TPCA decreased and that in CdTe(S)@PAI QDs increased slightly after mixing, which indicates the existence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer between TPCA and CdTe(S)@PAI QDs. The prepared probe showed two distinguishable emission bands at 420 and 525 nm under excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 4). As outlined in Scheme 1, the green fluorescence of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs with NH2 groups can be effectively and selectively quenched by Cu2+, whereas the blue fluorescence of TPCA moiety has no response to Cu2+, leading to an identifiable fluorescence colour change from green to cyan that can be applied for the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ (Fig. 4b, Scheme 1).

In addition, a slight red-shift from 525 to 530 nm was observed together with significant quenching of fluorescence. The red-shift may be ascribed to the formation of CdS+-Cu+ with lower energy level than CdS [44]. The effective quenching of Cu2+ should be the consequence of electron transfer from the cores to Cu2+ ions mdeated by amino groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the TPCA-CdTe@PAI QDs ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The stability is critical for the detection reliability of target analyte. Therefore, we studied the stability of the proposed complex systematically, and the results were shown in Figs. 5 and 6
. From Fig. 5a, we can seen that the change of the fluorescence ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) of the probe can be ignored with the continuous excitation light illumination for 100 min. And the TPCA-CdTe@PAI QDs also possess good salt tolerance; the fluorescence ratio was maintained under different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the probe aslo showed pretty robust response even at conditions seriously deviated from the selected one. From Fig. 6a we can see that the relative fluorescence intensity of proposed probe remains unchanged with ±20% of concentration change of the probe. Although temperature causes apparent change of fluoresence for most of dyes, the proposed complex probe show a constant ratio with a temperature range of 20–50 °C (Fig. 6b), which is suitable to be used outside of professional laboratories for the field analysis. Furthermore, the TPCA can be stored as crystal form for more than one year with no trait change and the CdTe(S)@PAI QDs solution can be stored more than two months without coagulation and fluorescent degradation.

The fluorescent TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI complex could be used for quantitative analysis of trace amount of Cu2+. Firstly, the experimental conditions including the incubation time and temperature were optimized carefully. From Fig. 7 a, we can seen that the fluorescence intensity ratio of TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI complex decreased fastly during the initial period and reached equilibrium after 3 min. Although the fluorescence ratio of the probe remains constant during the measurement, the incubation temperature after mixing of the probe and Cu2+ caused apparent change and the highest was attained at 40 °C (Fig. 7b). Higher temperature gave lower value, most probably because of the competing effects of Cu2+ with other components. Besides, phosphate buffer was used through the whole experiment to avoid the effect of pH change on the fluorescence of the complex probe.

Fluorescence spectra and the I
525nm
/I
420nm values versus different concentrations Cu2+ were shown in Fig. 8 . The TPCA-CdTe@PAI complex fluorescence probe initially exhibited an obvious emission peak at 525 nm. With the addition of Cu2+, an obvious decrease of fluorescence intensity at 525 nm can be observed conspicuously, which was probably because of the effective electron transfer from the QDs cores to Cu2+ with the aid of amino groups (Fig. 8a). As a contrast, the fluorescence emission of TPCA at 420 nm remained approximately constant during the process. The plot of I
525nm
/I
420nm value versus the Cu2+ concentrations is shown in Fig. 8b, it decreases linearly along with the Cu2+ concentration at a reasonable range, which could be applied to the quantitative analysis of Cu2+. The proposed probe was a complex with a concentration of 50 μmol·L−1 of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs (based on Te2− concentration) and 0.48 μmol·L−1 of TPCA, the curve of fluorescent ratio (I
525nm
/I
420nm ) vs. the concentration of Cu2+ was linear at a concentration range of 0.05–1.6 μmol·L−1. The correlation coefficient is about 0.997. Based on 3 times of standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N = 3), the limit of detection (LOD) can reach 24 nmol·L−1. Although there are previous reports that used specialized coatings on QDs for fluorescence detection of Cu2+ [29,35], those works mainly used small molecule modified QDs. Our in-situ formed polymer coating can be much more effective to prevent the release of the heavy metal ions as previous reported [41]. Compared with other reported probes, as shown in Table 1 , the sensitivity of the proposed complex probe is comparable. Furthermore, the LOD is much lower than the limit value of Cu2+ in drinking water (about 20 μM) according to the standard of U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [45]. Therefore, the sensitivity of the present method is high enough for the real water samples analysis.

The selectivity of the TPCA-CdTe(S)@PAI complex probe for Cu2+ was carefully evaluated against other common metal ions and anions. As shown in Fig. 9 , only Cu2+ can cause ratiometric response on the complex probe, which indicates the high selectivity of the probe. It should be mentioned that the concentrations of the co-existing ions reported here are not the highest tolerable concentrations; its tolerance can be much higher and may be used for samples with much more complicated matrix, although the focus of the present work is the Cu2+ contamination of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a novel association complex ratiometric fluorescence probe was designed for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ by simply mixing CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA. The fluorescence signal of CdTe(S)@PAI QDs can be selectively quenched by Cu2+, while the fluorescence intensity of TPCA is maintained in the presence of Cu2+ added. Meanwhile, the fluorescence colour can be changed from green to cyan. This probe exhibits high stability, good sensitivity and anti-interference capability for the detection Cu2+. The detection limit of 24 nmol·L−1 is good enough to be used for real samples. The obvious significance of this work is the simplicity of the probe construction and its robustness. The preparation processes for both CdTe(S)@PAI QDs and TPCA are green. The function of PAI may be multiple, it can reduce the chance of leakage of Cd2+ and improve the stability of the QDs, NH2 on its side chain may ensure the selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+, and this layer may also prevent the possible quenching of direct contact of CdTe(S) with TPCA. Because of the reactivity of NH2, it also provides a possibility to be tuned as sensors for other analytes simply through modification of NH2 groups within this layer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the scientific research platform of Lanzhou University, China.

Carbon quantum dot-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection

Mixed-solvothermal synthesis and applications in sensing for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions of flowerlike LaVO4:Eu3+ nanostructures

Highly selective and sensitive determination of copper ion based on a visual fluorescence method

Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for sensing Hg2+ and Ag+ as well visualization of latent fingerprints
Quantum dot bioconjugates for ultrasensitive nonisotopic detection
Colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals: the aqueous approach

One-step facile green synthesis of a highly fluorescent molecule through a way towards carbon dots and detection of dopamine based on in-situ formation of silver nanoparticles
Facile one-step photochemical synthesis of water soluble CdTe(S) nanocrystals with high quantum yields

Synthesis of a novel p-extended hybrid rhodamine dye with far-red fluorescence emission and its application in bioimaging

One-step microwave synthesis of carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions in aqueous solution
Highly selective electrochemical strategy for monitoring of Cerebral Cu2+ based on a carbon dot-TPEA hybridized surface
Carbon dots for copper detection with down and upconversion fluorescent properties as excitation sources
